title: From The Temporary To The Eternal Lessons From The Life Of The Apostle Paul
weekTheme: Boys play; men grow God has walked with you this year WHAT A YEAR! Can you believe it? This is the last week of year 1 of the Primal Path. Why don’t you take a moment to stop and celebrate. At this point, you have done over 250 daily devotions, are about to complete five difficulty days, answered hundreds of questions, and meditated through more sections of scripture than most grown men will do in their lifetime. WAY TO GO!!! As you stop to reflect, try to imagine what would have happened if you hadn’t been on this journey this year? You would just be drifting along like so many other guys your age without any idea or vision of what becoming a man is actually about. You would be another year older, but not necessarily another year wiser. Don’t rush past this. You have done something really, really extraordinary. You should hold your head high. This week we are going to recap the journey you have been on so far to retrace some of the high points and burn them in your heart for the journey ahead. We will reflect on the differences between boys and men, and why the five shifts will be so significant as you move ahead. You have completed the content of the five shifts, but now it's time to move from content to conviction. You have to resolve in your heart to live out these changes, to keep moving forward, and refuse to live in ease, selfish-

days.title: Ease To Difficulty
days.scripture: GENESIS 50:19-20 But Joseph replied, “Don’t be afraid of me. Am I God, that I can punish you? You intended to harm me, but God intended it all for good. He brought me to this position so I could save the lives of many people.
days.reflection: Boys want ease; men embrace difficulty. That’s how this whole journey began a year ago. The first shift you looked at was ease to difficulty, and the man you learned from was Joseph. One of the most important things we are trying to get you to take into account here in the Primal Path is the importance of thinking long- term and considering who you will be over the course of your life. Most guys your age tend to just think about high school first, then maybe a few vague thoughts about college, and then some distant thoughts about getting married and maybe having kids. But you need to see that God will be with you throughout your life and through all the challenges you will face. So, let’s recap how Joseph learned to shift from ease as a teenager to embracing the difficulty of helping lead and save a nation. Ease Joseph was blessed by his dad and was his favorite. He was given a sweet coat that let him know he was special and loved. He was safe, secure, and he had everything he wanted. Life was good. The difficulty of conflict God also gave him a vision, but he didn’t know how to handle it as a teenager. He shot his mouth off, and it got him in trouble with his brothers. They were jealous of him and felt he had a superiority complex. This caused some serious difficulty. The difficulty of circumstances Joseph was betrayed by his brothers and sold into slavery. They told his dad he was killed by wild animals. This is when Joseph realized to trust in God to help him get through life. He lands in the house of Potiphar, one of the most important men in Egypt. The difficulty of temptation God is with Joseph, and Potiphar’s wife wants to be too. She keeps trying to tempt him to have sex with her. He refused, dealing with temptation in a godly way. But this gets him thrown into prison. But sure enough, God is with him there. The difficulty of delays In prison, Joseph realizes God has given him the ability to interpret dreams. He does this for two of Pharaoh's men and thinks it’s his ticket out of there. But alas, he has to deal with delays and is forgotten in prison. The difficulty of discernment Pharoah has troubling dreams and none of his magicians can help him. Joseph is brought in and God reveals what is happening to him. He is then removed from prison and is made second in charge of the nation. The difficulty of responsibility Joseph has to use his leadership skills to store up food and provisions for the coming famine. God blesses his leadership with great favor, and the famine begins to spread. The difficulty of reconciliation Joseph's brothers come to get food, but don’t recognize him. He tests them to see if their hearts have changed, and at last, he forgives them and restores the relationship. The difficulty of change Joseph's family relocates to Egypt where they prosper, grow, and become a nation. The difficulty of legacy Joseph asks that his bones be carried into Egypt so he can be a part of God's larger story of redemption. Dang. A whole life in 10 points. But do you see the growth and the journey he went on as a man? God gave him a dream, empowered him to be faithful through horrific circumstances, and grew his character and capacity to be able to save a nation. What would have happened if Joseph gave into temptation, or refused to forgive his brothers, or denied God? His life would have been a disaster. Learning to reject ease and embrace difficulty is one of the most important things you can do at this age. It will set you up for influence, leadership, and a legacy that blesses others. Keep leaning in. Men who can handle difficulty are a gift to the world. Be one.
questions: In what ways have you seen a change in yourself where you are willing to embrace hard things rather than run from them in the last year?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they have seen you change, grow, and lean into hard things as you move forward into manhood. Ask them to be as specific as possible.
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how rejecting ease and embracing difficulty has helped them through this stage of their journey. What rewards have they seen from doing this?

thoughts.verse: It was by faith that Joseph, when he was about to die, said confidently that the people of Israel would leave Egypt. He even commanded them to take his bones with them when they left.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a vision of becoming a man who embraces and handles difficulty.

days.title: Self To Others
days.scripture: HEBREWS 11:23-29 It was by faith that Moses’ parents hid him for three months when he was born. They saw that God had given them an unusual child, and they were not afraid to disobey the king’s command. It was by faith that Moses, when he grew up, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh’s daughter. He chose to share the oppression of God’s people instead of enjoying the fleeting pleasures of sin. He thought it was better to suffer for the sake of Christ than to own the treasures of Egypt, for he was looking ahead to his great reward. It was by faith that Moses left the land of Egypt, not fearing the king’s anger. He kept right on going because he kept his eyes on the one who is invisible. It was by faith that Moses commanded the people of Israel to keep the Passover and to sprinkle blood on the doorposts so that the angel of death would not kill their firstborn sons. It was by faith that the people of Israel went right through the Red Sea as though they were on dry ground. But when the Egyptians tried to follow, they were all drowned.
days.reflection: David Gilmore has an interesting quote about one of the core elements of becoming a man. He wrote this: “Manhood is the defeat of childhood narcissism.” Do you know what that means? He is basically saying that when we are kids, all we think about is ourselves. We are self-centered and selfish. This is kind of natural when you are little; it’s how you survive and get a sense of who you are. However, becoming a man is about learning to go beyond yourself and maturing into someone who cares about others. A man who only cares about himself is toxically selfish. He will wound his friends and damage his family. He will leave a legacy of brokenness and pain. To become a man is to shift from having yourself at the center to having others at the center. This is the essence of greatness from a biblical perspective. According to Jesus, greatness is not defined by what you accomplish, but how you sacrifice for and serve others. This is a long journey, and a slow shift, but you must defeat the child inside you who refuses to grow up. We see a powerful example of this over the course of Moses' life. His life is a shining example of a man who shifts from living for himself to serving and rescuing a whole nation. Let’s recap and refresh this shift. Born for others Moses was born for a purpose. He was rescued from death and survived a genocide by the kindness of God. Prepared for others He was discovered in a river and raised in a palace as the son of Pharaoh’s daughter. He lived a life of privilege, power, and knowledge; perfectly positioned by God to be a leader for his people. Awakened for others All moments of change begin in a catalytic moment. Moses realized his own people were slaves of Pharoah, so he tried to liberate them in his own power. He murdered an Egyptian and had to flee because of it. Formed for others Moses spent forty years as a shepherd and father under Jethro, the Priest of Midian. He had forgotten his previous life. Fighting for others Yet, when he was ready, God called him from a burning bush, sending him back to Egypt to liberate his people from slavery and bring them into the promised land. Freedom for others God hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he refused to let the Israelites go. So, God sent ten plagues that crushed Egypt, culminating with the Passover meal and judgment of the firstborn. Pharoah relented, and the Israelites miraculously escaped. Patience with others But the children of Israel didn’t know how to live as free people. They complained against God and were riddled with fear; something you can kind of understand after 400 years of slavery. A covenant for others God enters into a covenant with Israel (kind of like a marriage), where He promises to protect and provide for them if they are loving and loyal to Him. Loyalty to others The Israelites test Moses' heart. The people grumble, complain, and rebel against God. Moses has to act as an intercessor between God and the people. He shows them he really loves them and is loyal to them through a whole series of ups and downs. Living for others Moses ends up missing out on the Promised Land because of his disobedience, showing us how important leadership is for a man. But he is remembered as one of the most important yet humble men to ever live. As a man, your maturity is measured by your sacrifice. Your greatness is measured by what you give. Learning to move beyond your own preferences, wants, and demands is at the heart of godly masculinity. Most guys your age will have a limited vision of living for others, but you have learned that biblical greatness is different than worldly greatness. As you keep growing, continue to move out of yourself to the needs of others. The world is dying for leaders like this. Resolve in your heart to become one.
questions: What were some of the biggest lessons you learned in the shift from self to others? Why do you think it's so hard for guys your age to wrap their heads around this? How does the culture sabotage them from making this shift?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share some ways they have seen you move out of selfishness and into loving others this past year. Ask them to be as specific as possible.

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share some of the joy they have found in serving others. Where do they wish they had been less selfish at your age and more focused on the needs of others?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. It was by faith that Moses’ parents hid him for three months when he was born. They saw that God had given them an unusual child, and they were not afraid to disobey the king’s command. It was by faith that Moses, when he grew up, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh’s daughter. He chose to share the oppression of God’s people instead of enjoying the fleeting pleasures of sin. He thought it was better to suffer for the sake of Christ than to own the treasures of Egypt, for he was looking ahead to his great reward. It was by faith that Moses left the land of Egypt, not fearing the king’s anger. He kept right on going because he kept his eyes on the one who is invisible. It was by faith that Moses commanded the people of Israel to keep the Passover and to sprinkle blood on the doorposts so that the angel of death would not kill their firstborn sons. It was by faith that the people of Israel went right through the Red Sea as though they were on dry ground. But when the Egyptians tried to follow, they were all drowned. Hebrews 11:23-29 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to fill you with a heart for others.

days.title: Whole To Part
days.scripture: MATTHEW 11:7-15 As John’s disciples were leaving, Jesus began talking about him to the crowds. “What kind of man did you go into the wilderness to see? Was he a weak reed, swayed by every breath of wind? Or were you expecting to see a man dressed in expensive clothes? No, people with expensive clothes live in palaces. Were you looking for a prophet? Yes, and he is more than a prophet. John is the man to whom the Scriptures refer when they say, ‘Look, I am sending my messenger ahead of you, and he will prepare your way before you.’ “I tell you the truth, of all who have ever lived, none is greater than John the Baptist. Yet even the least person in the Kingdom of Heaven is greater than he is! And from the time John the Baptist began preaching until now, the Kingdom of Heaven has been forcefully advancing, and violent people are attacking it. For before John came, all the prophets and the law of Moses looked forward to this present time. And if you are willing to accept what I say, he is Elijah, the one the prophets said would come. Anyone with ears to hear should listen and understand!
days.reflection: You live in a time of history when the world tells you that you should live for yourself. You are the most important one in the whole world. The culture is trying to get you to believe that you are at the center of the human story. As cool as you are, you are not the center of the human story; Jesus is. Learning to play your part, but not needing to have the starring role, is one of the most freeing and yet frustrating shifts a man has to make. John the Baptist lived in this tension better than any man who has ever lived. He knew he was a prophet with a significant role in the story of God, yet he didn’t demand to be at the center of the story. He did his part, turned the world to Jesus, then died as a martyr. Not a long life, but one that mattered. Let's recap how he showed us how to move from thinking the whole story is about us to simply playing our part. Setting the stage God is always looking to use men to accomplish His purposes in the world. He put John the Baptist in the perfect time and place to fulfill his calling. He has actually done the same thing for you. In the beginning John the Baptist was raised in a home that taught him to fear God, value the story of God, and care about the story of God's people. Are you actually in a similar home right now? How can you tell? You are doing the Primal Path. Finding your voice God gave John the Baptist a message of truth to stir the hearts of the people of God. It was a message of hope that caused them to lift their eyes from their small stories to God's larger story. Preparing the way John the Baptist called the people to get ready for God to move. He helped them create the conditions for Jesus to appear and bring the good news of his gospel to the world. Beholding the lamb John the Baptist was secure in his call, knowing his purpose and role was to shift the attention from himself to Jesus. The star of the show was here, and it wasn’t him. Jesus is better John knew that Jesus had come to bring a new relationship to God's people and save the world from its sin and brokenness. He did everything in his power to get people to embrace Jesus and follow Him. The cost of critique John used his power to confront evil and injustice. He was willing to do the right thing and was free from the fear of man. Wrestling with your role John learned that there is a cost to playing our part in the story of God. He was beheaded by Herod and faded from the stage of history when his part was done. The greatest and the least Jesus called John the greatest man who had ever lived, but He also said that the relationship we get with Jesus is better than what John had. You matter, and you are important, but you are not the center. This is the key to godly confidence. Calling and humility, playing your part, but not hogging all the attention. You are called to do something significant for God, but your main role is to make Jesus the center of your life and point to Him. No man is ever big enough to be the center of the world's attention except Jesus. Play your part, but point to Him. That’s what a real man does.
questions: What have you learned this past year about the kind of man you want to be? What have you learned about making sure you point to Jesus and not just yourself?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share some ways they have both seen you find your role as a young man and point past yourself to Jesus. Ask them to be specific.
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they have learned how to hold both confidence and humility in tension. How has this helped them navigate life as a man?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Not to us, O LORD, not to us, but to your name goes all the glory for your unfailing love and faithfulness. Psalm 115:1 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to continue to show you your role in His kingdom and give you a heart to use your life to point people to Jesus.

days.title: Control To Surrender
days.scripture: PSALMS 78:70-72 He chose his servant David, calling him from the sheep pens. He took David from tending the ewes and lambs and made him the shepherd of Jacob’s descendants—God’s own people, Israel. He cared for them with a true heart and led them with skillful hands.
days.reflection: We live in a world of control freaks. Insecure men trying to control everything in their lives. They try to control what happens to them, try to control others, and try to manipulate others to get what they want. But what this reveals is a lack of trust in God. People who are unwilling to obey God end up trying to play God and often make things worse; driving people away from them and disobeying what God wants. This has painful and heartbreaking consequences over the course of a man’s life. King David showed us another way. He was willing to trust God in the major areas of his life. He surrendered himself to the wisdom and timing of God. This didn’t make him perfect, but it made him a man of faith—a man after God's own heart. Let’s recap the shift from control to surrender he modeled in his life. Control to surrender King Saul was a control freak who tried to take things into his own hands. He was somebody who failed to trust God in the most pivotal moments of his life. As a result, God removed the kingdom from him and gave it to David, a man He could trust. Surrendering attention David was overlooked by Samuel to be the king because Samuel was judging by human standards. But God looked at the heart, and David had found God’s attention. Surrendering fear David was sent by his father to care for his brothers who were in the middle of a battle against the Philistines. Goliath was tormenting the Israelites, and everyone was afraid. But David stepped up, surrendered his fear, and ended up killing Goliath. This was the start of his rise to power. Surrendering relationships David built an amazing friendship with Saul’s son, Jonathan. Saul opposed this friendship, and they were separated because of King Saul’s fear and control. Surrendering comfort This led King Saul to hunt David down out of jealousy. He tried to kill him and was obsessed with eliminating threats to the throne. David trusted God and went into the wilderness for years, living as a fugitive even though he was the future king. Surrendering timing David was given a chance to kill King Saul on two separate occasions, but he refused to because he was waiting for God to make him king, He didn’t want to take the throne through human timing, but God’s timing. He didn’t force or rush things out of ambition. He waited out of surrender. Surrendering reputation The first thing David did when he was appointed king was to bring the Ark of the Covenant into Jerusalem. He wanted to prioritize the presence of God and make it the defining mark of his leadership. He danced before the ark with all his might, and was mocked for his passion. David didn't care. He was a man after God's own heart. Surrendering sin David got too casual about his leadership and fell into sin. He cheated on his wife with Bathsheba, murdering her husband and covering it all up. When God confronted him through the prophet Nathan, he repented from his sin and left the consequences in God’s hands. He trusted in His mercy and grace. Surrendering family David was a terrible father. He did a lot of things right, but he failed to raise his kids properly. As a result, his kids murdered one another and betrayed him, and it broke his heart. He thought his future was doomed, but God had mercy on him and continued His promises through David’s son Solomon. Surrendering legacy David was worried that his sin and failures may block his legacy, but God was faithful in spite of all he had done. He was remembered as a man after God’s own heart and for the things he got right. Jesus was a part of David’s lineage, proving that, despite anything we do wrong, God can still redeem our lives. As you continue to grow into a man, you will have to learn to surrender. As you get older and realize the complexities of life, you will have to make a decision to rely on God and not hustle with human strength to make things happen yourself. Hopefully, you have gotten a sense over the past year that it's worth waiting for God to work instead of taking things into your own hands. The reason you can trust God is that He loves you and has a plan and purpose for your life. Don’t try to shortcut that because you will only sabotage yourself. Don’t be a control freak; be a man of faith. God can do more than you can if you surrender it all to Him.
questions: Are there any areas of your life that you are still struggling to trust God in? Would you be willing to share about that at all?
What do you think are a couple of the hardest things for guys your age to be willing to surrender to God?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share some of the ways they have learned to trust God and not be a control freak. How have they seen God bless their trust and obedience?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Trust in the LORD with all your heart; do not depend on your own understanding. Seek his will in all you do, and he will show you which path to take. Don’t be impressed with your own wisdom. Instead, fear the LORD and turn away from evil. Then you will have healing for your body and strength for your bones. Proverbs 3:5-8 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God for grace to continue surrendering your life to Him.

days.title: Temporary To The Eternal
days.scripture: 2 CORINTHIANS 4:16-18 That is why we never give up. Though our bodies are dying, our spirits are being renewed every day. For our present troubles are small and won’t last very long. Yet they produce for us a glory that vastly outweighs them and will last forever! So we don’t look at the troubles we can see now; rather, we fix our gaze on things that cannot be seen. For the things we see now will soon be gone, but the things we cannot see will last forever.
days.reflection: You have spent the last year studying the lives of some of biblical history's greatest men. You need mentors and models to look at to try and figure out what sort of man you want to be in the world today. This brings us to the life of the apostle Paul. Paul was a man seized with a vision of Jesus. He was a man who understood how to live well. He was a man who understood how to shape history through the gospel. God wants you to put away childish things so that you can enjoy better things; this was the heart of Paul’s message. He knew that Jesus taught us the best way to think about the core issues of life, sex, power, money, faith, heaven, hell, and eternity. Paul knew Jesus came to save us from trivial things that don’t matter and only disappoint us. Paul wanted us to experience life in all its fullness. So, let’s look at his life and recap his insights on how a man can shift his vision from the temporary to the eternal. The biggest shift a man can make Most guys your age are obsessed with the “here and now.” But as you grow into a man, you will have to look beyond the things that seem important to the things that actually are important. This happens when you encounter Jesus and His vision of the kingdom. Jesus and eternity Shifting to an eternal vision is not just about shifting how you think about time; it's shifting what you think matters most in life. This is what happened when Paul met Jesus. He saw that Jesus really was the Son of God, the Savior of the world, and worth giving everything else for. In every generation, men have an encounter with Jesus that changes them forever, and that’s been the goal of the Primal Path this past year. Sex and eternity Most guys your age think about sex purely from a physical perspective. They think about their body and girl's bodies. They don’t see the larger purpose of sexuality in light of God’s larger story. Sex is a gift from God designed with a purpose in mind. Using it out of context does damage. Jesus doesn’t want to spoil sex; He wants to protect and elevate it to its proper place so it blesses rather than damages men. Money and eternity Money is a gift to be used wisely to serve God’s kingdom purposes. But it can become a trap that makes us think about storing up stuff for the here and now. Paul wanted us to see money as something to be stewarded and not something to be selfish with. He wanted us to send our wealth ahead so we could enjoy it forever, not squander it on stupid stuff now. Power and eternity Because most secular people think that this life is all there is, they tend to hold onto power and use it for their own personal advantage. But because we know we have eternal life, we are free to use our power to serve others and encourage them to live for Jesus. Being a servant and not a psycho with power is a sign of a secure man. Time and eternity What we do in life echoes in eternity. Paul wanted us to use our time wisely on the things that really matter. We can waste our time, use our time, invest our time, or redeem our time. Boys live like they have all the time in the world; men live like it’s a precious resource that must be spent wisely so they don’t miss out on opportunities or waste their lives. Hell and eternity We are eternal beings, not just meat suits we walk around in until we die. Paul had a heart of love and wanted people to experience the freedom, joy, and gift of eternal life that Christ came to give us. He warned people against the dangers of hell so they could experience life and be safe from the consequences and punishment of their sin. Heaven and eternity The Bible’s view of heaven is not clouds, robes, and harps. It’s cities, rewards, glory, joy, and intimacy with God. Having a huge eternal ‘yes’ helps us say ‘no’ to the things that don’t matter now. Rewards and eternity Men can sometimes think about everything they seemingly miss out on to follow Jesus. This is a false perspective. Everything we do as a disciple of Jesus will be richly rewarded. Paul wasn’t trying to get us to miss out on the good things of life; he was trying to prepare us for the glorious things of the kingdom of God. A wise man will live for the greater rewards. Men live for the temporary or the eternal. What will you choose? The temporary things of this life may seem fun in the moment, but they will be over before you know it and leave you empty and wanting more. But living for eternity is about living for the things that bring us the deepest joy and satisfaction. It’s about living for what really matters. It’s about a vision of life with Jesus that gets better and better and better and better. Don’t be a fool and build your life on the sand with a house that collapses under the storm. Build on an eternal foundation of the grace of Jesus, and you will be richly rewarded in the days to come.
questions: What are some temporary things that you used to think were amazing but now have outgrown and can see don’t really matter?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they have seen you making wiser decisions and lifting your eyes off of the temporary and the trivial. Ask them to be as specific as possible.
Ask the person you are doing this study with how their vision of eternity shapes how they live now. What are they saying ‘no’ to now so they can say ‘yes’ later?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Yes, everything else is worthless when compared with the infinite value of knowing Christ Jesus, my Lord. For his sake I have discarded everything else, counting it all as garbage, so that I could gain Christ and become one with him. I no longer count on my own righteousness through obeying the law; rather, I become righteous through faith in Christ. For God’s way of making us right with himself depends on faith. I want to know Christ and experience the mighty power that raised him from the dead. I want to suffer with him, sharing in his death, so that one way or another I will experience the resurrection from the dead! Philippians 3:8-11 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you fix your mind on things above and not get caught in the distractions of the here and now. Ask Him to stamp eternity on your eyes.

extras: WEEK 1 0 MANSCHOOL NO MAN SCHOOL No man school this week. Make sure to prepare for Difficulty Day.
